content.text: Why the Baseline Is the Hardest Part

Most GenAI benefits realisation failures are not caused by the technology underperforming — they are caused by teams launching without a signed pre-launch baseline. Without one, there is no agreed starting point, so any post-launch measurement can be disputed. The business owner claims the baseline was better than it was; the programme team claims the improvement is larger than it is. The baseline resolves this dispute before it starts.

The baseline is not 'the number we had last quarter'. It is a documented, agreed, independently verifiable snapshot of the current process, measured consistently, signed by the business owner, and locked before any GenAI system touches production traffic.

Measuring the baseline is expensive — it requires 4–8 weeks of clean data collection, stakeholder alignment, and sometimes instrumentation work to extract metrics that nobody was tracking. That cost is the minimum entry price for defensible benefits realisation.

attrs.alt: Five-step benefits baseline process: identify metrics, locate data sources, measure pre-launch values over 4-8 weeks, validate with business owner, lock signed baseline
attrs.title: Benefits Baseline Measurement Process

content.text: The five-step process above treats the baseline as a deliverable with its own definition of done: a signed document co-owned by the PM and the business owner, locked before the GenAI system enters production testing. The baseline table in the diagram shows a real-world example for a customer support GenAI initiative.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

class BenefitCategory(Enum):
    EFFICIENCY   = "efficiency"      # time/cost reduction
    QUALITY      = "quality"         # error rate, accuracy, CSAT
    CAPACITY     = "capacity"        # volume handled without headcount growth
    REVENUE      = "revenue"         # incremental revenue or retention
    RISK         = "risk"            # compliance, safety, error reduction

class MeasurementUnit(Enum):
    MINUTES      = "minutes"
    PERCENT      = "percent"
    CURRENCY_GBP = "gbp"
    HOURS        = "hours"
    COUNT        = "count"
    SCORE        = "score"           # e.g. CSAT 1-5

@dataclass
class BenefitMetric:
    id: str
    name: str
    category: BenefitCategory
    unit: MeasurementUnit
    data_source: str                 # system that holds the raw data
    extraction_query: str            # SQL/API/report that produces the value
    measurement_window_days: int     # how many days of history to average
    target_direction: str            # "increase" or "decrease"

@dataclass
class BaselineMeasurement:
    metric: BenefitMetric
    value: float
    measurement_start: date
    measurement_end: date
    sample_size: int                 # number of records/events in the window
    confidence_note: str             # any caveats about data quality
    measured_by: str
    validated_by: str                # business owner who signs off
    locked_on: date
    target_value: float              # agreed post-launch target
    target_date: date                # when the target should be achieved

    @property
    def target_delta_pct(self) -> float:
        if self.value == 0:
            return float("inf")
        return (self.target_value - self.value) / self.value * 100

    def is_signed(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self.validated_by and self.locked_on)

    def summary_line(self) -> str:
        direction = "+" if self.target_delta_pct > 0 else ""
        return (
            f"{self.metric.name}: {self.value} {self.metric.unit.value} "
            f"→ target {self.target_value} ({direction}{self.target_delta_pct:.0f}%) "
            f"by {self.target_date}"
        )
Not every metric in the business case is measurable before launch. Apply four selection filters to determine which metrics qualify for the baseline:

: the metric must change in a way that can be attributed to the GenAI system, not to external factors (seasonality, headcount changes, product changes). If you cannot control for confounds, the metric is not suitable for benefits realisation.
: the data source must exist and be extractable today. If the metric requires new instrumentation, either build it before the baseline window opens or remove it from the baseline.

: the metric must be stable during the baseline window — not undergoing a major process change or system migration. Measuring a metric while the underlying process is changing produces a noisy baseline.
: the business owner must accept the metric definition, the data source, and the calculation method. Disputed metric definitions become disputed results.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class MetricQualificationResult:
    metric_id: str
    attributable: bool
    measurable_now: bool
    stable: bool
    agreed: bool
    disqualification_reason: str = ""

    @property
    def qualifies(self) -> bool:
        return all([self.attributable, self.measurable_now, self.stable, self.agreed])


def qualify_metrics(
    candidates: list[BenefitMetric],
    check_attribution: Callable[[BenefitMetric], bool],
    check_data_source: Callable[[BenefitMetric], bool],
    check_stability: Callable[[BenefitMetric], bool],
    agreed_metrics: set[str],   # metric IDs signed off by business owner
) -> list[MetricQualificationResult]:
    results = []
    for m in candidates:
        attr = check_attribution(m)
        meas = check_data_source(m)
        stable = check_stability(m)
        agreed = m.id in agreed_metrics
        reason = ""
        if not attr:
            reason = f"Cannot isolate GenAI contribution from external factors"
        elif not meas:
            reason = f"Data source '{m.data_source}' not accessible"
        elif not stable:
            reason = f"Process undergoing change during baseline window"
        elif not agreed:
            reason = f"Business owner has not signed off on metric definition"
        results.append(MetricQualificationResult(
            metric_id=m.id,
            attributable=attr,
            measurable_now=meas,
            stable=stable,
            agreed=agreed,
            disqualification_reason=reason,
        ))
    return results


def filter_qualified(results: list[MetricQualificationResult]) -> list[str]:
    """Return IDs of metrics that pass all four filters."""
    return [r.metric_id for r in results if r.qualifies]

The baseline measurement window must be long enough to average out normal variation but short enough to reflect current operating conditions. The standard is 6–8 weeks for weekly-cycle processes and 4 weeks for daily-cycle processes.
Quarterly sales cycle must be captured
Baselining Metrics That Do Not Yet Exist

Some GenAI initiatives target benefits that the organisation has never measured before — for example, 'time spent on manual data extraction' or 'proportion of emails requiring a follow-up'. These benefits are real but the baseline requires
: sample a representative set of work items, time each step manually over 2–4 weeks, and derive an average. This is expensive but unavoidable if the benefit is to be claimed.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from statistics import mean, stdev

@dataclass
class TimeAndMotionSample:
    activity: str
    duration_minutes: float
    complexity: str     # "low", "medium", "high"

def derive_baseline_from_samples(
    samples: list[TimeAndMotionSample],
    confidence_threshold: int = 30,   # minimum samples for a reliable baseline
) -> dict:
    """
    Compute a baseline value from a set of manually-timed samples.
    Returns mean, std dev, and a confidence flag.
    """
    if not samples:
        raise ValueError("No samples provided")
    durations = [s.duration_minutes for s in samples]
    avg = mean(durations)
    sd = stdev(durations) if len(durations) > 1 else 0.0
    return {
        "baseline_mean_minutes": round(avg, 1),
        "std_dev": round(sd, 1),
        "coefficient_of_variation": round(sd / avg if avg else 0, 2),
        "sample_count": len(samples),
        "confidence": "high" if len(samples) >= confidence_threshold else "low",
        "caveat": (
            f"Based on {len(samples)} samples — "
            + ("sufficient for reliable baseline." if len(samples) >= confidence_threshold
               else f"increase to {confidence_threshold} before locking baseline.")
        ),
    }


# Example: manual document extraction activity
samples = [
    TimeAndMotionSample("extract key fields from contract", 12.5, "medium"),
    TimeAndMotionSample("extract key fields from contract", 9.0,  "low"),
    TimeAndMotionSample("extract key fields from contract", 18.0, "high"),
    TimeAndMotionSample("extract key fields from contract", 11.0, "medium"),
    TimeAndMotionSample("extract key fields from contract", 14.5, "medium"),
]
result = derive_baseline_from_samples(samples)
print(result)
# {'baseline_mean_minutes': 13.0, 'std_dev': 3.4,
#  'coefficient_of_variation': 0.26, 'sample_count': 5,
#  'confidence': 'low', 'caveat': 'Based on 5 samples — increase to 30 ...'}

The baseline is only valid if it cannot be retroactively revised. A signed baseline document with a named business owner and a lock date is the minimum governance artefact. Store it in a version-controlled location accessible to the PMO.
the PM (technical accountability) and the business owner (business accountability). Finance should co-sign any baseline with a revenue or cost impact above £50k.

the metric definition, the data source, the calculation method, the measurement window, the baseline value, and the target value. None of these can change post-launch without a formal change request signed by the same parties.
version-controlled document in the programme SharePoint or Confluence, linked from the business case and the benefits register. Never in a personal email or local file.

quarterly post-launch, the benefits owner compares actuals to the locked baseline and reports the delta. If the baseline itself was wrong (e.g., a data error is discovered), a formal baseline amendment is raised — never a silent update.

The greatest threat to a credible baseline is a confound — a change that occurs during the measurement window or immediately after launch that also affects the metric. Common confounds in GenAI programmes include seasonal demand variation, headcount changes, product feature releases, and pricing changes. Document all known confounds in the baseline record and plan how to account for them in post-launch analysis.
Extend window to span the same season; or normalise by volume
New agents hired during baseline period
Measure per-agent metrics, not absolute totals
New self-service feature reduces ticket vol.
Segment tickets by type; exclude self-service-deflected
Sales incentive inflates Q4 deal velocity
Use LTM average rather than single-quarter baseline

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ConfoundRecord:
    name: str
    description: str
    start_date: date
    end_date: date
    affected_metrics: list[str]   # metric IDs that may be impacted
    severity: str                 # "low", "medium", "high"
    control_method: str           # how the analysis will account for it

@dataclass
class BaselineConfoundLog:
    """
    Attached to a BaslineMeasurement to document what was happening
    in the environment during the measurement window.
    """
    programme: str
    window_start: date
    window_end: date
    confounds: list[ConfoundRecord] = field(default_factory=list)

    def high_severity_confounds(self) -> list[ConfoundRecord]:
        return [c for c in self.confounds if c.severity == "high"]

    def affects_metric(self, metric_id: str) -> list[ConfoundRecord]:
        return [c for c in self.confounds if metric_id in c.affected_metrics]

    def baseline_reliability(self) -> str:
        """
        Assess overall baseline reliability based on confound severity.
        High-severity confounds require explicit acknowledgement in the baseline document.
        """
        high = len(self.high_severity_confounds())
        if high == 0:
            return "high"
        if high == 1:
            return "medium — one high-severity confound; document control method"
        return f"low — {high} high-severity confounds; consider extending window or deferring baseline"


# Example confound log
log = BaselineConfoundLog(
    programme="genai-customer-support",
    window_start=date(2025, 1, 6),
    window_end=date(2025, 2, 28),
    confounds=[
        ConfoundRecord(
            name="Post-holiday demand surge",
            description="January historically sees 25-30% higher ticket volume than the rest of the year",
            start_date=date(2025, 1, 6),
            end_date=date(2025, 1, 31),
            affected_metrics=["aht", "fcr", "escalation_rate"],
            severity="medium",
            control_method="Compare week-of-year to prior year; normalise by ticket volume",
        ),
    ],
)
print(f"Baseline reliability: {log.baseline_reliability()}")
print(f"Confounds affecting AHT: {[c.name for c in log.affects_metric('aht')]}")

The confound log should be included in the signed baseline document alongside the metric values. Post-launch analysis must re-check each logged confound to determine whether it is still active and whether the control method was effective. If a new high-severity confound appears post-launch (e.g., a recession), a formal baseline amendment may be needed rather than silently adjusting the attribution.

A signed pre-launch baseline is the non-negotiable foundation of benefits realisation. It requires identifying attributable and measurable metrics, collecting 4–8 weeks of pre-launch data, validating both the data source and the value with the business owner, and locking the result in a governed document before production deployment. Without a locked baseline, post-launch benefit claims are unverifiable and programme ROI calculations are contested rather than trusted.
